I've never been to the Seattle stadium myself but use to watch a lotta seahawks games, especially when they were in their prime defensively. I've just always liked defense more than offense in sports and appreciate watching a good D play

But that stadium is truly something else in terms of noise and atmosphere

has registered as 2.0 earthquakes on the richter scale from fans while having 137.6 decibals 

This is louder than a jet engine taking off from close range (which is about 120 to 130 dB) 

Should be a fun game to watch monday

tweets.text:
He also said @Strategy will never sell BTC.

It didn't quite turn out like that:

→ Dec 2022: sold 704 BTC for tax-loss harvesting, then bought back even more (legit strategy)

→ May 2026: sold 32 BTC to fund STRC preferred stock dividends (a sale to meet cash obligations

Also, on June 29, Strategy formally authorized a BTC Monetization Program.

This basically lets the company sell BTC over time to fund reserves, dividends, interest, and buybacks.

It's best to stop believing what ultra-rich people say.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: 🔥 UPDATE: Strategy aims to keep $STRC trading between $99 and $100 over time, says Michael Saylor. https://t.co/ycJJ7DMHWu
tweets.full_text:
He also said @Strategy will never sell BTC.

It didn't quite turn out like that:

→ Dec 2022: sold 704 BTC for tax-loss harvesting, then bought back even more (legit strategy)

→ May 2026: sold 32 BTC to fund STRC preferred stock dividends (a sale to meet cash obligations

Also, on June 29, Strategy formally authorized a BTC Monetization Program.

This basically lets the company sell BTC over time to fund reserves, dividends, interest, and buybacks.

It's best to stop believing what ultra-rich people say.

tweets.text: I didn't realize this till recently, but the math section of the SAT is a de facto second English section. For whatever reason they can't give you problems that are mathematically difficult, so the only way to add difficulty is to make the problems hard to read.

thread_tweets.text: I didn't realize this till recently, but the math section of the SAT is a de facto second English section. For whatever reason they can't give you problems that are mathematically difficult, so the only way to add difficulty is to make the problems hard to read.
thread_tweets.full_text: I didn't realize this till recently, but the math section of the SAT is a de facto second English section. For whatever reason they can't give you problems that are mathematically difficult, so the only way to add difficulty is to make the problems hard to read.

thread_tweets.text: I wonder if they did this deliberately because making the math section of the SAT actually about math would have demographic consequences they don't want to see, or if it just happened by accident due to other random constraints.
thread_tweets.full_text: I wonder if they did this deliberately because making the math section of the SAT actually about math would have demographic consequences they don't want to see, or if it just happened by accident due to other random constraints.

thread_tweets.text: @matthew_d_green There are a lot of problems that aren't explicitly taught in high school but that don't require more than high school math to figure out. E.g. the sum of the first hundred integers. Is there some reason they couldn't use problems like that to spread out the high end of the field?
thread_tweets.full_text: @matthew_d_green There are a lot of problems that aren't explicitly taught in high school but that don't require more than high school math to figure out. tweets.text: There is something to this. There's definitely a "buck stops here" element to being a founder. But I think it's going slightly too far to say it's your number one job. Your number one job is to solve the important problems, and pain and importance are not perfectly correlated.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
As a founder, your number one job is to eat shit.

You're on the call with the upset customer. You need to fire people by telling them directly. You're listening to the engineer who wants to quit due to burnout. You have to read the 40-page vendor contract, which is a nightmare
tweets.full_text: There is something to this. There's definitely a "buck stops here" element to being a founder. But I think it's going slightly too far to say it's your number one job. Your number one job is to solve the important problems, and pain and importance are not perfectly correlated.

tweets.text:
News out! 🚨  
Drills are now turning at Myriad Uranium's Copper Mountain project.
$M.CN
At a market cap of only ~US$40M, Myriad offers exposure to what has the potential to become the largest #uranium project in the U.S.

The first four holes could get the program off to a flying start. They begin at Lucky Cliff, where historical drilling intersected uranium mineralization from depths as shallow as 20 ft (6 m). A standout historical hole returned 355 ft grading 0.027% eU₃O₈. 

In its maiden drill program at Copper Mountain, Myriad exceeded historical results, so I wouldn't be surprised to see one of these first holes deliver another standout intercept. 

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
News Alert 📣 July 2, 2026
Myriad Uranium Commences Phase II Drilling at Copper Mountain

Read here⬇️
https://t.co/coW72ihwrh

$M.CN $MYRUF #uranium #nuclearenergy https://t.co/TdfOA8vybs
tweets.full_text:
News out! 🚨  
Drills are now turning at Myriad Uranium's Copper Mountain project.
$M.CN
At a market cap of only ~US$40M, Myriad offers exposure to what has the potential to become the largest #uranium project in the U.S.

The first four holes could get the program off to a flying start. They begin at Lucky Cliff, where historical drilling intersected uranium mineralization from depths as shallow as 20 ft (6 m). A standout historical hole returned 355 ft grading 0.027% eU₃O₈. 

In its maiden drill program at Copper Mountain, Myriad exceeded historical results, so I wouldn't be surprised to see one of these first holes deliver another standout intercept. 

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
Prediction:

The most successful projects of the next few years won’t be built on super innovative concepts, fancy narratives or anything of that sort.

It will be the ones that:

1) generate real revenue

2) have a clean structure with either just tokens or just equity, not both

Almost sounds too simple to be true. But the reality is that competition is so underwhelming that ticking these two boxes, which should be taken for granted, is enough to make you stand out as exceptional.

Big opportunity in that gap that won’t exist forever.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Tokens with equity do not work https://t.co/SsXfeSA5xh

tweets.text:
There's $1.2T of Bitcoin sitting in wallets right now.

Less than 1% of it earns anything.

Compare that with 28% of $ETH and 65% of $SOL already earning yield through staking.

Most BTC just sits in wallets doing nothing, which is kinda wild when you think about the size of it.

Been looking at how Stacks approaches this with Dual Stacking.

You can hold sBTC and earn around 0.5% APY, or pair it with $STX through Dual Stacking and boost that up to around 5% APY in sBTC.

The key difference is that the rewards stay BTC-denominated. No points program or random token airdrops. You're just earning more of the thing you already hold.

One thing to keep in mind is that the higher yield comes with STX exposure, so your actual outcome can change if STX moves against you.

There are a few other small signals stacking up too.
STX also showed up in Coinbase's COIN50 index recently. 

It's a small thing, but it's another sign that Bitcoin DeFi is slowly becoming its own category instead of an afterthought.

None of these is a massive catalyst on its own.

The interesting part is the direction...the largest pool of idle capital in crypto is slowly getting reasons to do something.

Glad to partner with @Stacks to highlight how Bitcoin yield is starting to move beyond just holding.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
With Bitcoin Staking on Stacks, your BTC stays locked on Bitcoin L1 under your own keys. You get it back because it was always yours.

From our fireside chat with @Bitwise👇 https://t.co/EuLXg9DBYH

The TSLA stock reaction to deliveries (down 6%) buries the lede. The EV winter is ending and deliveries still matter.

https://t.co/iEndprirML
tweets.full_text:
The TSLA stock reaction to deliveries (down 6%) buries the lede. The EV winter is ending and deliveries still matter.

https://t.co/iEndprirML

tweets.text:
$TSLA shares down 6% on the huge delivery beat for three reasons. The biggest was buy on the rumor. The second is a question of how much did high gas prices help the number and third, the ending of the DOGE headwind (Elon left in May 25).

Bottom line: The EV winter that started in March of 2024 is ending. Even backing out those one time benefits, it still was a monster delivery number, likely up 20% plus vs. up 6% in March.

tweets.text:
$TSLA down 2% on the monster delivery numbers.  I think this is mostly buy on the rumor, sell on the news.  Stock is still up 12% over the past week compared to the Nasdaq up 4%. 

The whisper must have been much higher than I realized. 

I can't find anything sinister in the numbers. They missed energy deployments by 3%, but investors are even less focused on energy compared to delivers. 

More to come.

tweets.text:
$TSLA deliveries are the first sign we're exiting the EV winter that started in March of 2024.

June Deliveries were up 25% y/y (vs. the Street up 6% and the high-end estimate of 21%). This was the strongest delivery number since Sep'23, which was up 27% y/y. Additionally, June Deliveries would have been up 27% if not for the S/X sunset.

My guess is higher gas prices worldwide likely impacted deliveries by 5%. In other words, the strength in deliveries tells us something bigger is going on. That is Tesla's fundamentally the most appealing EV on the market (in the U.S.) and more broadly, consumers are returning to EVs.

Deliveries still matter because more vehicles means more shots on goal for autonomy.

tweets.text:
"Pokemon" is short for "pocket monsters".
"Digimon" is short for "digital monsters".

Therefore, "salmon" is short for "saltwater monsters".

(You might object: salmon spend a big part of their life in freshwater. But pokemon spend a big part of their life outside pockets too!)

tweets.text:
We actually found $INSM by accident while researching $ABVX when it was trading around $95(now at $138).

As part of our due diligence, we spoke with our biotech expert and started digging into who the smartest shareholders were. One name immediately stood out: Darwin Global. They had roughly 9% of their portfolio invested in $ABVX.

When I asked our biotech hedge fund expert about them, his response was simple: “That’s probably the best biotech hedge fund of the last five years.” If I remember correctly, he said they compounded their portfolio by roughly 10x through a handful of highly concentrated bets.

Today, around 68% of Darwin Global’s reported equity portfolio is invested in $INSM, with $ABVX representing another major position.

That doesn’t prove the investment thesis is right, of course. But when a specialist biotech fund with that kind of track record is willing to make such an outsized bet, my assumption is that they’ve done an extraordinary amount of work. That was one of the reasons we decided to dig much deeper into the company ourselves.

tweets.text:
Rotation is everything. It doesn’t matter how cheap a stock looks. 

If the market decides to rotate into another sector, money will follow that rotation. Cheap stocks can stay cheap, and expensive stocks can keep getting more expensive. 

Don’t fight the flow of capital. Follow where the market is sending it.
tweets.full_text:
Rotation is everything. It doesn’t matter how cheap a stock looks. 

If the market decides to rotate into another sector, money will follow that rotation. Cheap stocks can stay cheap, and expensive stocks can keep getting more expensive. 

Don’t fight the flow of capital. Follow where the market is sending it.

tweets.text:
The NASDAQ is 97.5% correlated to total global liquidity.

It has almost nothing to do with earnings or how good the companies are, and everything to do with how much money the world's central banks are printing.

The small slice that liquidity doesn't explain is the NASDAQ's own adoption curve sitting on top.

This is why everyone keeps saying equities are expensive. They're using a measure that stopped working the moment we started debasing currency. A high valuation doesn't really tell you a company is doing well anymore, it tells you how much money has been printed.

This is the heart of my Everything Code framework. Once you understand that liquidity is the key driver of all asset prices, the market stops behaving like a mystery and starts running like clockwork.

tweets.text:
By checks, I mean people who check on other people to make sure they're performing well, and by balances, I mean balances of power. Even the most benevolent leaders are prone to becoming more autocratic, if for no other reason than because managing a lot of people and having limited time to do it requires them to make numerous difficult choices quickly, and they sometimes lose patience with arguments and issue commands instead. And most leaders are not so benevolent that they can be trusted to put the organization's interests ahead of their own.
#principleoftheday

tweets.text:
For our 10th season, we are celebrating the 250th year of the USA by concentrating the entire Meet the Drapers season on the US. We've already taken Meet the Drapers through Tuscaloosa, Alabama, Detroit, Michigan, New York City, New York, Nashville, Tennessee, Austin, Texas, Salt Lake City, Utah, and Honolulu, Hawaii this season.

Each stop is better than the one before. Word has gotten out in the US about the show and we are getting more and better contestant entrepreneurs for the show.

The next generation of world-changing companies is being discovered, created and vetted through new models like Meet The Drapers.

In this Meet The Drapers episode, I got to share the stage with my son Adam Draper (Boost VC), former U.S. Treasurer Rosie Rios, and James Walker and the energy was incredible.

tweets.text: I am in Europe and am surprised how conservative people are with regards to AC. They literally make up reasons why they don't want to install it (going from hot to cold makes you sick, sleeping in AC is not healthy), even in a heat wave! They have no clue what they are missing.

tweets.text:
I'm pretty convinced @saylor is gonna announce that because as long as people know he'll hit the $STRC ATM at $100, it automatically creates a ceiling for all the people buying the dip for the upside to $100

No reason to hold to $100 if you're certain it can't go above it, so everyone frontruns that level and it's not reached unless a ton of demand for yield appears.

The same thing goes for shorts, they are guaranteed a free lunch with the ATM at $100, so there's no way Strategy keeps it this way

The ATM should be hit anywhere from $100 to $110 at the discretion of the issuer
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
One no-brainer improvement for $STRC and $SATA (but it obviously won't be very useful in the near-term) is to let it float above $100, maybe to $110

STRC is trying to defy the laws of physics by having both a stable price and a quasi-stable yield (in the sense that the yield

thread_tweets.text:
.@PunterJeff @Werkman @hilllery__dan All of you should probably agree on the ATM being hit at $100 being a mistake. The target range for STRC/SATA should be wider and centered at $100.
Limiting the upside above $100 means the average price of STRC will always be strictly below $100 which is a gift for shorts and not beneficial at all for the holders

tweets.text:
One very simple reason the Fed and Treasury can’t afford to raise interest rates and why the inflationistas are likely to be proven wrong.  

From Torsten Slok:

“A wave of corporate bonds issued during the low-rate era of 2020 and 2021 is now coming due, forcing companies to refinance cheap debt at today's higher rates, with high-yield borrowers feeling the squeeze first given their shorter 5-to-8-year maturities, while investment-grade issuers sit in a stronger position today, having locked in low rates with 10-year-plus tenors that push their refinancing needs comfortably further out.”

Pair this with the refinancing needs of over $1 trillion at the Treasury and I highly doubt will see a hawkish Fed’s

Credit spreads rising should keep expectations for any rate rising at bay. 

@infraa_
